MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus/Cocos & Callison-Burch 2016, Syn.VSM, and AddCos) and evaluates them on the same tasks/datasets (CoInCo, PPDB, etc.) using consistent metrics (B-Cubed F-Score and GAP). It explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better (e.g., TWSI outperforms on noun-only rankings) and discusses practical limitations (best-fit WSD bottleneck, PPDB thresholding). The authors analyze differences in coverage and substitution performance (showing improved GAP and coverage over WordNet and prior clustered paraphrase inventories) and cite relevant competing approaches, making the comparisons comprehensive and fair. | B(5): The paper evaluates against appropriate and recent baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus/Cocos & Callison-Burch 2016, Syn.VSM, and AddCos) and uses the same datasets and metrics (CoInCo, PPDB2.0, B-Cubed F-Score and GAP) for fair comparison. It explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform better (TWSI on nouns) and discusses practical limitations (best-fit WSD bottleneck, PPDB thresholding), while clearly analyzing differences introduced by SubstClus (multi-view NMF with AddCos, PPDB, translations, WordNet views) and reporting hyperparameters and selection criteria. Related work coverage is described as adequate, so comparisons are comprehensive and fairly presented.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a focused metric (B-Cubed F-Score adapted for substitutability) and a multi-view clustering system (SubstClus) that non-trivially combines contextual AddCos signals, PPDB2.0 paraphrase strengths, shared translations, and WordNet synset affinities via NMF and silhouette-based k selection. These contributions clearly differentiate the work from prior PPDB-based clustering (e.g., PPDBClus) and produce inventories with higher coverage and better lexical-substitution filtering, so the techniques represent a notable, well-motivated extension rather than a purely incremental combination of known pieces. Given the concrete gains reported and the tailored evaluation for substitutability, the results would likely be informative to experts in lexical substitution and sense induction. | B(4): The paper contributes a notable, clearly differentiated approach: it adapts B-Cubed F-Score to evaluate substitutability and introduces SubstClus, a multi-view NMF-based clustering that combines Context Substitutability (AddCos), PPDB paraphrase strengths, shared translations, and WordNet synset affinities. Compared to prior work (e.g., PPDBClus, TWSI, Syn.VSM), the method explicitly optimizes for substitutability and demonstrates improved inventories for lexical substitution, which indicates a meaningful, non-trivial extension. However, most individual components are established techniques (AddCos, PPDB, NMF, silhouette-based k selection), so the novelty lies primarily in their integration and tailoring rather than in wholly new algorithms.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work addresses lexical substitution and sense-inventory construction—core topics in computational semantics—by proposing a substitutability-focused B-Cubed F-score and a multi-view clustering method (SubstClus) that integrates contextual AddCos, PPDB, translations, and WordNet. It evaluates on standard resources (CoInCo, PPDB2.0, TWSI, WordNet) against appropriate baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus) using accepted metrics (B-Cubed F, GAP, Silhouette), matching the methods and evaluation standards expected at top NLP/ML venues. The stated limitations (weaker noun performance, WSD bottleneck) are candid and do not reduce relevance; overall the paper is well positioned for a top-tier NLP/ML conference audience. | B(5): This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it tackles lexical substitution and sense-inventory construction with a novel multi-view clustering approach combining AddCos, PPDB2.0, shared translations, and WordNet, and evaluates on standard resources (CoInCo, PPDB-XXL, WordNet, TWSI). The methodology (multi-view NMF, silhouette-based k selection) and evaluation (B-Cubed F-score, GAP) and baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, AddCos) match community expectations. Reported limitations (weaker noun performance, WSD best-fit bottleneck) are acknowledged but do not undermine the paper’s clear relevance to the venue’s audience.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments are fairly comprehensive: SubstClus is evaluated on multiple datasets (CoInCo, PPDB2.0, AGiga, WordNet, TWSI), compared to appropriate baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos), and assessed with multiple metrics (B-Cubed F-Score, GAP, Silhouette). The paper reports hyperparameters and examines practical issues (oracle vs. best-fit filtering) and explicit limitations (weaker noun performance, WSD bottleneck, PPDB threshold tradeoff), which supports reproducibility and honest failure analysis. However, the summary does not indicate explicit ablation or sensitivity studies for the multi-view components or for choice of thresholds (beyond selecting k by silhouette), and code availability is not mentioned, leaving some minor gaps for full reproduction and deeper analysis. | B(4): The paper presents broad, multi-faceted experiments: evaluations on CoInCo and PPDB-derived inventories, comparisons to WordNet/TWSI/PPDBClus/Syn.VSM, and metrics that include B-Cubed F-Score and GAP; clustering choices (k via silhouette) and PPDB threshold trade-offs are reported. It also discusses limitations (weaker noun performance, best-fit WSD bottleneck) and reports hyperparameters, which supports reproducibility. Missing from the summary is an explicit ablation over the individual clustering views (though some sensitivity—e.g., PPDB threshold and k selection—is analyzed) and code availability is not mentioned, so a few important reproducibility/analysis gaps remain.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are fairly comprehensive: SubstClus is evaluated on multiple datasets (CoInCo, PPDB2.0, AGiga, WordNet, TWSI), compared to appropriate baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos), and assessed with multiple metrics (B-Cubed F-Score, GAP, Silhouette). IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets the important problem of producing sense inventories and evaluation metrics specifically optimized for lexical substitutability, proposing an adapted B-Cubed F-Score and a multi-view clustering method (SubstClus) that combines Context Substitutability (AddCos), PPDB2.0, shared translations, and WordNet. It empirically yields inventories with greater coverage than WordNet and outperforms prior paraphrase-clustering approaches at improving lexical substitution rankings (GAP), which should change how researchers construct and evaluate substitutability-oriented senses. By demonstrating both practical gains and clear bottlenecks (weaker noun performance, best-fit WSD failure, and PPDB threshold trade-offs), it opens promising directions for follow-up work on sense-specific WSD and multi-view inventory construction that are likely to be built upon in the subfield. | B(4): The paper addresses an important, practical problem—creating sense inventories aligned to human substitutability—and proposes both a targeted evaluation metric (B-Cubed F-Score for substitutability) and a novel multi-view clustering method (SubstClus combining AddCos, PPDB2.0, shared translations, and WordNet). Empirical results showing greater coverage than WordNet and improved lexical-substitution rankings (GAP) over prior PPDB-based inventories indicate the work will change how researchers build and evaluate task-tailored sense inventories. The demonstrated bottleneck in best-fit WSD and limitations on nouns mean the contribution is strong but not yet transformative; it nonetheless opens clear new directions for task-focused sense induction and multi-view inventory creation that others are likely to build on.

It empirically yields inventories with greater coverage than WordNet and outperforms prior paraphrase-clustering approaches at improving lexical substitution rankings (GAP), which should change how researchers construct and evaluate substitutability-oriented senses. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: SubstClus uses a reasonable multi-view NMF clustering (AddCos, PPDB2.0, translations, WordNet), k selection via Silhouette, and evaluations on CoInCo and PPDB with appropriate baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus) showing improved GAP under oracle/best-fit sense filtering. There are no substantive mathematical derivations required and the methodology is sound overall, though the summary does not report statistical significance testing or code release which would strengthen reproducibility. Given the acknowledged limitations (weaker noun performance, WSD best-fit bottleneck, PPDB threshold/coverage tradeoff) the conclusions are justified and the issues are minor, so the work is technically solid apart from these small gaps. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are supported by empirical evidence: experiments compare SubstClus to appropriate baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos) on relevant datasets (CoInCo, PPDB2.0, Gigaword) and use sensible metrics (B-Cubed F, GAP) showing improved coverage and substitution performance. There are no complex mathematical proofs to verify and the described NMF-based multi-view clustering methodology is plausible. Methodology is generally sound, but the summary does not report statistical significance testing and the choice of k via mean Silhouette (rather than directly optimizing substitutability) is a potential minor methodological concern. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, with only minor issues that do not undermine the main findings.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: SubstClus uses a reasonable multi-view NMF clustering (AddCos, PPDB2.0, translations, WordNet), k selection via Silhouette, and evaluations on CoInCo and PPDB with appropriate baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus) showing improved GAP under oracle/best-fit sense filtering. There are no substantive mathematical derivations required and the methodology is sound overall, though the summary does not report statistical significance testing or code release which would strengthen reproducibility. Given the acknowledged limitations (weaker noun performance, WSD best-fit bottleneck, PPDB threshold/coverage tradeoff) the conclusions are justified and the issues are minor, so the work is technically solid apart from these small gaps.

There are no complex mathematical proofs to verify and the described NMF-based multi-view clustering methodology is plausible. Methodology is generally sound, but the summary does not report statistical significance testing and the choice of k via mean Silhouette (rather than directly optimizing substitutability) is a potential minor methodological concern. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear experimental narrative (intro → multi-view NMF method → datasets and baselines → evaluations with B-Cubed F-score and GAP → limitations). The main contributions (B-Cubed F-Score for substitutability and the SubstClus multi-view clustering combining AddCos, PPDB2.0, translations, and WordNet) are stated early and carried through the experiments. Notation and experimental details appear consistently reported (hyperparameters, Silhouette selection, PPDB thresholds), and figures/tables/examples seem informative given the detailed methodological and evaluation summary. Overall writing quality is good, with only minor presentation issues around emphasizing the noun-specific weaknesses and the practical WSD bottleneck that could be highlighted more prominently. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early (B-Cubed F-Score for substitutability; the SubstClus multi-view clustering method). Notation and methodological details seem consistently presented (metrics like FB3_beta, GAP, Silhouette Coefficient and hyperparameters are reported), and the experimental comparisons (baselines, datasets, evaluation metrics) suggest figures/tables and examples are informative. Writing quality is described as good, though a few presentation choices could be tightened (e.g., clearer emphasis/visualization of the WSD best-fit bottleneck and noun-specific weaknesses).

main_claims: Proposes B-Cubed F-Score as a cluster-quality metric to measure how well a sense inventory matches human substitutability judgments for lexical substitution.

Introduces a multi-view clustering method (SubstClus) to produce sense clusters tailored for substitutability, combining Context Substitutability (AddCos), Paraphrase Similarity (PPDB2.0), Shared Translations, and WordNet Synsets views.

Produces sense inventories with greater coverage than WordNet and that outperform previous automatically clustered paraphrase inventories (e.g., Cocos and Callison-Burch 2016 / PPDBClus) at improving lexical substitution rankings when used for sense-based filtering.

Demonstrates that filtering lexical substitution rankings by sense clusters can substantially improve generalized average precision (GAP), though practical WSD (best-fit selection) remains a bottleneck).
_summary.methodology: Automatically cluster PPDB paraphrases per target using a multi-view NMF-based clustering that ingests multiple views (contextual AddCos scores over many sentences, paraphrase-paraphrase PPDB2.0 scores, shared translations, and WordNet-synset affinities), select k by maximizing mean Silhouette Coefficient over k∈[2,10], and evaluate substitutability with extended B-Cubed F-Score and lexsub improvements via GAP-based oracle and best-fit sense filtering.
datasets: Turk Bootstrap Word Sense Inventory v2 (TWSI) (used as a baseline sense inventory)
baselines: TWSI (Turk Bootstrap Word Sense Inventory v2)
AddCos (Melamud et al., 2015) used as a ranking model/baseline ranking
limitations: SubstClus inventories are weakest for nouns; TWSI outperforms them on noun-only rankings.
The simple best-fit WSD method often fails to find the correct sense cluster, introducing bias and limiting realized improvements.
Applying a PPDB score threshold (2.3) reduces noise but also decreases coverage of the resulting sense inventory.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - lexical substitution; sense inventory/sense clustering for paraphrases
